coredata.dc:description: Nanozymes sterilizing drug-resistant (DR) bacteria by generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) have emerged as a promising approach for treating infected wounds. However, designing biocompatible adaptive ROS balance therapeutic platform for DR bacteria-infected wounds remains a great challenge. Herein, biocompatible lignin-based carbon dot nanomaterials (LCDs-OA) with more CO and COOH functional groups on the surface were prepared by hydrothermal organic acid co-processing and used as a dual enzyme-like active nanozyme for the therapy of wounds infected by DR bacteria. Experimental and theoretical calculations demonstrated that the CO and COOH functional groups in LCDs-OA have site-switching roles in the dual enzyme-like activity, which can balance reactive oxygen species (ROS) according to the changes in microenvironmental pH at the wound site, and realize the adaptive regulation of ROS generation for bactericidal and ROS scavenging for healing. Furthermore, the performance differences between different organic acid treatments were investigated and an adaptive ROS balance therapeutic platform (LCDs-OA@CaO2) was constructed to overcome the major limitation in treating DR bacterial infected wounds. The system achieved >99.6 % in vitro antimicrobial efficiency against DR bacteria and was highly biocompatible while effectively reducing oxidative damage to normal tissues, resulting in satisfactory therapeutic results in repairing wounds infected with DR bacteria.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The morphology and structure of oxalic acid treated lignin carbon dots (LCDs-OA). (a) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images, the corresponding dots diameter distribution histogram from 100 LCDs-OA. (b) Demonstrates imaging obtained using an Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) with an inset that reveals the vertical height distribution of a particular line in (b). (c) Raman spectra of the CDs. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the CDs. The C1s spectra of (d) LCDs; (e) LCDs-OA. (f) Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of AL (yellow curve); LCDs (blue curve) and LCDs-OA (red curve), respectively.

Peroxidase-like (POD-like) and catalase-like (CAT-like) activity of materials. (a) Typical absorption spectra of TMB colorimetric reaction with different materials. (b) Reaction-time curves of TMB colorimetric reaction. (c) Typical absorption spectra of TMB colorimetric reaction in different pH buffer solutions. (d) Reaction-time curves of TMB colorimetric reaction in different pH buffer solutions. The relative activity of LCDs and LCDs-OA in different (e) pH and (f) temperature. (g) Electron spin resonance (ESR) experiments of LCDs-OA. (h) Comparison of •OH. Characterization for CAT-like activity of LCDs and LCDs-OA. Effect of (i) time and (j) concentrations on the oxygen generation from H2O2 decomposition. (k) Oxygen generation catalysis in different pH buffer solutions effect of time. (l) Oxygen generation catalysis by LCDs and LCDs-OA in different pH buffer solutions after 10 min incubation.

Enzyme-like activity and structural characterization of materials treated in different ways. (a) Typical absorption spectra of TMB colorimetric reaction with different materials. (b) Reaction-time curves of TMB colorimetric reaction. (c) CAT-like activity studies of materials treated in different ways. (d) The corresponding relative activity histogram. (e) FT-IR spectra of materials treated in different ways. The C1s XPS spectra of (f) LCDs-OA-NaBH4; (g) LCDs-OA-NaOH; (h) LCDs-OA-PS.
Plots of the energy distribution of H2O2 conversion under different conditions. In acidic buffer (a) −C; (b) −CO; (c) –COOH. In alkaline buffer (d) −C; (e) −CO; (f) –COOH.

In vitro antibacterial studies. (a)–(b) Typical photographs of MDR E. coli and MDR S. aureus after the treatment with different condition. (c)–(d) Typical photographs of MDR E. coli and MDR S. aureus after the treatment with different concentrations of LCDs-OA for 4 h. Bacterial viability of MDR E. coli and MDR S. aureus were evaluated with (e) different condition and (f) different concentrations of LCDs-OA by UV–vis spectroscopy method. (g) The TEM images of MDR E. coli and MDR S. aureus before and after treatment with LCDs-OA at 40 μg·mL−1 for 4 h.

In vitro antioxidant capacity of LCDs-OA, LCDs-OA@CaO2. Viability of NCM-460 cells treated with different concentrations of (a) LCDs-OA and (b) LCDs-OA@CaO2 for 24 h. Cell viability of NCM-460 cells treated with 1 mM H2O2 for 24 h in the presence of different concentrations of (c) LCDs-OA and (d) LCDs-OA@CaO2. (e) Fluorescence images of NCM-460 cells stained by Calcein AM/PI under different treatment conditions. Scale bar is 200 μm. Data are expressed as mean ± s.d. of three independent experiments. ***p < 0.001; ns, not significant, one-way ANOVA.

ROS scavenging activity of LCDs-OA@CaO2 in vitro. (a) Fluorescence images of NCM-460 cells stained with Hoechst 33342/DCFH-DA under different treatments. (b) Fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) results showing ROS levels in NCM-460 cells over 24 h under different treatment conditions. The scale bar is 200 μm. Data are expressed as mean ± s.d. of three independent experiments. ***p < 0.001 compared to the H2O2-treated group alone.

Wound disinfection and healing efficacy of LCDs-OA@CaO2. (a) Representative macroscopic appearance of DR bacteria infected wounds treated with three methods (n = 4): Ⅰ: PBS buffer, Ⅱ: CaO2, Ⅲ: LCDs-OA@CaO2. (b) Body weights of DR bacteria infected mice in each group. (c) Relative mean wound area in DR bacteria infected mice 10 days after therapy. (d) Representative photographs of H&E (Scale bar: 100 μm) and Mason (Scale bar: 20 μm) staining of dermal wounds in both groups, Ⅰ: PBS buffer, Ⅱ: CaO2, Ⅲ: LCDs-OA@CaO2. (e) H&E staining images of major organs of mice in both groups of Ⅰ: PBS buffer, Ⅱ: CaO2, Ⅲ: LCDs-OA@CaO2. Scale bar: 200 μm. Data are expressed as mean ± s.d. of four independent experiments. **p < 0.01, one-way ANOVA.
Mechanism of healing promotion and adaptive ROS generation/scavenging process in bacterially infected mouse wounds treated with LCDs-OA@CaO2.

head.ce:title: Self-adaptive carbon nanozyme regulation of ROS balance for bacteria-infected wound therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A biocompatible adaptive ROS balanced therapeutic platform (LCDs-OA@CaO2) was constructed to overcome the major limitations in treating DR bacterial infected wounds, and satisfactory therapeutic results were achieved in repairing wounds infected by DR bacteria.

Nanozymes sterilizing drug-resistant (DR) bacteria by generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) have emerged as a promising approach for treating infected wounds. However, designing biocompatible adaptive ROS balance therapeutic platform for DR bacteria-infected wounds remains a great challenge. Herein, biocompatible lignin-based carbon dot nanomaterials (LCDs-OA) with more CO and COOH functional groups on the surface were prepared by hydrothermal organic acid co-processing and used as a dual enzyme-like active nanozyme for the therapy of wounds infected by DR bacteria. Experimental and theoretical calculations demonstrated that the CO and COOH functional groups in LCDs-OA have site-switching roles in the dual enzyme-like activity, which can balance reactive oxygen species (ROS) according to the changes in microenvironmental pH at the wound site, and realize the adaptive regulation of ROS generation for bactericidal and ROS scavenging for healing. Furthermore, the performance differences between different organic acid treatments were investigated and an adaptive ROS balance therapeutic platform (LCDs-OA@CaO2) was constructed to overcome the major limitation in treating DR bacterial infected wounds. The system achieved >99.6 % in vitro antimicrobial efficiency against DR bacteria and was highly biocompatible while effectively reducing oxidative damage to normal tissues, resulting in satisfactory therapeutic results in repairing wounds infected with DR bacteria.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Numerous lives have been saved by the discovery of antibiotics, which are now mostly effective against bacteria by interfering with their membranes, protein synthesis, and DNA replication. [1,2] However, with the passage of time, inappropriate use and over-reliance on antibiotics have led to the rapid spread of antibiotic resistance (AMR), making the problem of drug-resistant (DR) microorganisms more significant. [3,4] The emergence of DR bacteria has made it even more difficult for modern medicine to treat infected wounds. [5] Bacterial infection wound healing is a very complex biological process involving antimicrobial, inflammatory response, proliferation and tissue reconstruction. [6–11] At the initial stage of wound therapy, antibacterial are needed to improve wound healing. After reducing bacterial infection, reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels need to be controlled and adequate oxygen needs to be provided to accelerate cell proliferation/collagen synthesis and promote tissue regeneration [6–9,12].

In order to reduce the use of antibiotics to avoid AMR, nanozymes have attracted much attention in the biomedical field as multifunctional and highly stable nanomaterials. [13–17] Currently developed nanozymes materials with peroxidase (POD)-like activity can can catalyze the production of ROS from physiological concentrations (50–100 μM) of H2O2 to kill bacteria (including DR bacteria). [18–24] However, the residual H2O2 after the bacteria have been eliminated, as well as excess ROS, can inhibit the formation of connective tissue, leading to delayed wound healing. [25–27] Nanozymes with catalase-like (CAT) activity were found to scavenge excess ROS and convert H2O2 to O2, thereby reducing the effects of H2O2 on tissues while relieving tissue hypoxia. [28–31] On this basis there are a small number of researches to achieve antibacterial and ROS scavenging compound effects in therapy by compounding multiple enzyme-performing materials.[32,33] Or by external intervention methods (e.g. Sono-piezodynamic therapy, Photothermal, etc.) to switch the enzyme-like activity of nanozymes to realize the balanced regulation of ROS. [32,34] Therefore, the development of a simple and efficient biocompatible nanozyme material to achieve ROS adaptive balance without the introduction of external intervention and composite materials is of great significance for the infected wounds therapy.

Existing research has shown that the enzyme-like activity of nanozymes can be altered or improved by changing or increasing the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the nanozymes. [35–37] Lignin is a naturally aromatic biomass, is rich in benzene ring structure and surface functional groups, which can realize efficient electron transfer while providing abundant active sites. [38–42] The abundant sp2 hybridized benzene ring structure in lignin makes it the best precursor for assembling carbon dots (CDs). [43] retreatment techniques, such as acid hydrolysis and ultrasonic treatment, are commonly used to promote the hydrothermal treatment of lignin. [44] Acids are multifunctional in that they not only act as “top-down” scissors, but also as oxidizing agents, thus integrating additional sp2 hybridized domains into the carbon core of CDs. [43] Organic acid treatment of lignin leads to the formation of functionalized lignin fragments, and organic acids not only increase the CO and COOH functional groups on the surface of lignin, but also act as a second carbon source to promote the polymerization of benzene rings into carbon dots. [45–47] Therefore, the use of organic acid hydrothermal treatment of lignin to synthesize carbon dots is expected to improve biocompatibility while enabling the design and synthesis of multiactive site nanoenzymes, which can be used as adaptive wound microenvironmental nanomedicines for infected wound therapy.

Herein, biocompatible lignin-based carbon dot nanomaterials (LCDs-OA) with more CO, COOH functional groups on the surface were prepared by our hydrothermal organic acid co-treatment method and used as a dual enzyme-like active nanozyme for the therapy of wounds infected by DR bacteria. (Scheme 1 ) It was demonstrated that at lower pH (4.5–6.5) in the bacterial infection microenvironment (BME), it possesses POD-like activity that decreases progressively with increasing pH and catalyzes the formation of ROS hydroxyl radicals (•OH) from H2O2 to kill DR bactera. [48–50] It possesses progressively enhanced CAT-like activity in the pH range (6–10) to decompose ROS (H2O2) into H2O and O2. Surface state-dependent nanozyme activity experiments and DFT calculations showed that this adaptive enzyme-like activity stems from the shift of the major active site from CO to COOH as the pH increases during wound healing, enabling LCDs-OA to balance ROS at different stages of wound healing in DR bacterial infections. Finally, we composite CaO2 (CaO2 can provide H2O2 under BME conditions and O2 under physiological conditions) and LCDs-OA together to realize a biocompatible adaptive ROS balanced therapeutic platform (LCDs-OA@CaO2) with smart H2O2/O2 self-sufficiency. To this end, we developed an adaptive ROS balance therapeutic platform (LCDs-OA@CaO2) using lignin, which overcomes the main limitations of treating infected wounds with DR bacteria (e.g., DR bacteria, hypoxia, and limited H2O2 availability), [51–53] with minimal effect on normal tissue cells. Meanwhile, the research of different enzyme activity sites is of great significance for the subsequent development of carbon materials utilizing surface functional groups to provide multiple enzyme activities.

LCDs was prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method. In a typical synthesis, alkali lignin is dissolved with 20 mL of distilled water in a 50 mL beaker. After stirring for 30 min, transfer to Teflon high-pressure reactor and heat at 180°C for 12 h. The 0.22 μm filter membrane was vacuumized and the liquid product was collected and then extensively dialyzed with distilled water dialysis tubes (molecular weight 2000 Da was retained) for 2 days. The resulting yellow solution is then freeze-dried to make LCDs powder.

In a typical synthesis, alkali lignin is dissolved in a 50 mL beaker with 20 mL of distilled water, after which the same mass of oxalic acid dihydrate is added. After stirring for 30 min, it is transferred to a Teflon high pressure reactor and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. A 0.22 μm filter membrane is evacuated to collect the liquid product. The 0.22 μm filter membrane was evacuated and the liquid product was collected and extensively dialyzed (molecular weight retention 2000 Da) with distilled water dialysis tubing for 2 days. The resulting yellow solution was then freeze-dried to make LCDs-OA powder. LCDs-TA and LCDs-CA were synthesized by replacing oxalic acid dihydrate with tartaric acid and citric acid, respectively.

First of all, based on the early research technology, we started the preparation of CaO2. In this process, we first added 1.2 mL of 1strength ammonia-water solution (NH3-H2O) and 12 mL of polyethylene glycol 200 (PEG200) gradually to the stirring. Dissolve 3 g, equivalent to 2.7 mmol, in 3 ml of purified water. Next, 1.5 ml of 30 % hydrogen peroxide was introduced into the solution at a rate of 3 drops per minute and stirred for two hours at body temperature. Subsequently, NaOH (at a concentration of 0.1 mol/L) was added and stirring was continued until the pH of the solution was raised to 11.5. At this time, the formation of a white solid precipitate was observed, which was collected separately using filter paper. The precipitate was then rinsed with NaOH solution and the process was repeated until the pH of the washings dropped to 8. Thereafter, a washing action was performed with the aid of ethanol to remove the precipitate, followed by a dehydration treatment at 80 degrees Celsius for three hours in a vacuum atmosphere. After mixing LCDs-OA with CaO2 in equal proportions, the mixture was stirred in an ethanol-containing medium for up to 24 h with rotation, aiming to form a compound of LCDs-OA@CaO2.Finally, LCDs-OA@CaO2 was successfully prepared by centrifugation and washing with ethanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterizations of LCDs-OA nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para:
Herein, a method for synthesizing biocompatible dual enzyme-like active carbon dot nanozyme materials with oxygen-rich functional groups via hydrothermal organic acid treatment of lignin is presented. The introduction of oxalic acid provides more CO and COOH functional groups for lignin carbon dots (LCDs-OA). As shown in Fig. 1
a, the morphology and size distribution of LCDs-OA were studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The synthesized LCDs-OA exhibited a dot shape with an average diameter of 3.9 ± 0.4 nm (based on 100 LCDs-OA). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) mapped the obvious lattices of LCDs-OA; the lattice spacing distribution is approximately 0.22 nm, which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphite. [36] Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of LCDs-OA with an estimated average height of 5.1 ± 1.8 nm (Fig. 1 b). The deviation of the height of LCDs-OA measured by AFM from the diameter measured by TEM is due to the low contrast of the polymer structure. [54] A broad diffraction peak was discovered in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of lignin-based carbon dot (LCDs) and LCDs-OA ( Fig. S1 ). This peak is located near 2θ = 26 ◦, which belongs to the (002) face of graphite [Powder Diffraction File (PDF Card No. 01-0640)], which is consistent with the TEM results [35].

By fitting the Raman spectra, several band: SL, DS, D, K, GL, G, and Carbonyl were obtained in both LCDs and LCDs-OA (Fig. 1 c). [55,56] These peaks correspond to smaller polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, small molecules such as pyrene, CC vibrations of defects in the graphite structure, the Kekulé band, carbon sp2 stretching of arene rings with E2G symmetry, the stretching of sp2 carbons, and carbonyl band, respectively. [55,56] Peak position recording and peak area integral calculations were performed ( Table S1 ), demonstrating that more carbonyl groups were introduced after organic acid treatment. The introduction of strong electron withdrawing groups caused varying degrees of red shifts in the D, K, GL, and G band, and also resulted in a decrease in the K band. [56] By calculating ID/IG, we found that the ID/IG value of LCDs-OA (0.55) was higher than LCDs (0.51). This increase may be due to the introduction of more oxygen-containing functional groups, thereby enhancing the disorder of the LCDs-OA carbon framework. [57,58] Meanwhile, the introduction of organic acids increased the proportion of small molecular polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. Organic acids are verified that effectively break down lignin, producing more small molecular fragments that can polymerize into carbon dots.

The elemental composition and chemical states of both LCDs and LCDs-OA were investigated through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS surveys depicted in Fig. S2 a&b reveal that LCDs-OA exhibit a higher elemental oxygen content compared to LCDs. Detailed analysis of the high-resolution C 1 s spectra for both LCDs (Fig. 1 d) and LCDs-OA (Fig. 1 e) allows for deconvolution into four distinct components: CC, C-O, CO, and O-CO. [35,36] Through peak area integration, it becomes evident that LCDs-OA possess higher concentrations of O-CO, CO, and CC, while exhibiting lower levels of C-O in comparison to LCDs. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of alkaline lignin (AL), LCDs, and LCDs-OA are depicted in Fig. 1 f. In the FT-IR spectra, the peaks at 3437, 1449, and 850 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of –OH, H-O-H, and the out-of-plane vibration of O-H, respectively. [59,60] Comparisons revealed that the hydrothermal organic acid treatment of LCDs-OA had low levels of several –OH, while the carbon dot treatment only resulted in a decrease in the O-H of the out-of-plane vibrations. It is noteworthy that the peaks at 1045 and 1154 cm−1 originate from the C-O stretching vibrations while the C-O peaks of LCDs-OA were significantly weaker than those of ALs and LCDs, which suggested that there is a decrease in the presence of C-O in LCDs-OA.[35,60] In contrast, a distinct new peak appeared at 1708 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of LCDs-OA, indicating the presence of CO vibration. [36,47,59] The 1600 cm−1 peak corresponds to CC stretching vibration, indicating higher CC content in LCDs-OA compared to AL and LCDs. [35,36] With potassium hydrogen phthalate (PBP) as the reference substance, the content of carboxylic acid and alcoholic hydroxyl groups in the surface layer of the material can be accurately determined by 1H Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (1H NMR) spectroscopy. ( Fig. S3 ). [35,36] The contents of reactive carboxyl groups on AL, LCDs and LCDs-OA were calculated to be 1.12, 0.78 and 1.42 mmol/g, respectively, and the contents of hydroxyl groups were calculated to be 5.01, 4.21 and 1.26 mmol/g, respectively. All results are consistent with XPS analysis, verifying that LCDs-OA have more CC, CO and O-CO and less C-O. The introduction of oxalic acid not only cleaves the β-aryl ether bond (β-O-4) of lignin, providing the necessary protons for hydrogen transfer and dehydrogenation of hydrocarbon reactants, but also the involvement of oxalic acid as a second carbon source during polymerization into carbon dots reduces phenolic hydroxyl groups while increasing CO and COOH ( Fig. S4 ). [43,46].

The POD-like activities of initial AL and synthesized LCDs, LCDs-OA nanozymes were evaluated using 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as substrate. In Fig. 2
a, LCDs-OA exhibited excellent peroxidase-like properties and its maximum absorption peak at 652 nm in the presence of H2O2 indicated that TMB was efficiently oxidized. Fig. 2 b shows that LCDs-OA displays a higher reaction rate within ten minutes compared to AL and LCDs, which may be attributed to the increase in oxygenated functional groups on the surface of LCDs-OA and the more extensive Π-conjugated system formed by CC. Whereas, the improved performance of LCDs compared to AL is attributed to the exposure of more surface functional groups after the synthesis of carbon dots. Key enzymatic parameters, constant (Km) and maximum reaction velocity (Vmax), were calculated using the classical Michaelis-Menten equation and Lineweaver-Burk model (Fig. S5 a-d). The affinity and reaction velocity of LCDs-OA for both substrates surpass those of LCDs and most other reported nanozyme materials (Table S2). Notably, the POD-like activity of LCDs-OA nanozymes was assessed using chromogenic substrates 2,2′-Azinobis-(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulphonate) (ABTS) and 1,2-diaminobenzene (OPD). In the presence of H2O2 and LCDs-OA, characteristic peaks of oxidation products were observed, confirming the POD-like properties of LCDs-OA (Fig. S6 a). The time-dependent absorbance further validated this (Fig. S6 b), establishing LCDs-OA as an ideal POD-like candidate. Testing TMB colorimetric reactions in pH = 4.5 and 7.4 buffer solutions revealed that LCDs-OA exhibited minimal POD-like activity under neutral conditions (pH = 7.4). Additionally, the absorbance over time at 652 nm showed that the reaction rate of LCDs-OA was higher at pH = 4.5 compared to neutral conditions (Fig. 2 c&d). Investigating the POD-like activities of LCDs and LCDs-OA at different pH (Fig. 2 e) and temperature (Fig. 2 f) demonstrated that LCDs-OA consistently outperformed LCDs. The highest activity was observed at pH = 4.5 and 45 °C, and POD-like activities decreased with increasing pH, which opens up the possibility of subsequent adaptive therapy. To further illustrate the POD-like properties of LCDs-OA and study its generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), we used 5,5-Dimethyl-1-Pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) and 4-Amino-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) as probes, with methanol as a quencher for •OH. With the addition of DMPO and without a quencher, •OH was produced by LCDs-OA via H2O2 activation. O2 •- was not observed when methanol was added to quench the •OH, and 1O2 production was not observed with the addition of TEMP as a probe (Fig. 2 g). Finally, the experiments were repeated without the addition of H2O2, and characteristic peaks of any radicals were not observed (Fig. S7 a). Meanwhile, MeOH was added to test the absorbance change in the system, and it was found that TMB could not be oxidized after quenching •OH (Fig. S7 b). This sufficiently proved that LCDs-OA activates H2O2 to generate •OH through POD-like properties to achieve TMB oxidation. Comparing the intensities of •OH peaks in the system, it was evident that LCDs-OA produced more •OH than LCDs (Fig. 2 h), explaining the differences in the absorbance of the TMB colorimetric reaction.

The CAT-like activity of AL, LCDs and LCDs-OA, efficiently catalyzing the decomposition of H2O2 to produce O2, was confirmed through the analysis of dissolved O2 content in the system (Fig. 2 i-l). Examining the relationship between time (Fig. 2 i), material concentration (Fig. 2 j), and dissolved oxygen concentration revealed that LCDs-OA demonstrated higher efficiency in dissolved oxygen output compared to AL, LCDs, suggesting higher CAT-like activity. This may be attributed to the increase in oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of LCDs-OA and the broader Π-conjugated system formed by CC. Key enzymatic parameters, including Km and Vmax under CAT-like activity, were calculated ( Fig. S8 ). Notably, LCDs-OA exhibited better affinity and reaction rate for H2O2 compared to LCDs and most reported nanozyme materials ( Table S3 ). This enhanced performance may be attributed to the presence of more oxygen-containing functional groups on LCDs-OA's surface and a more abundant Π-conjugated system formed by CC. Dissolved oxygen concentration changes in the presence of LCDs-OA and H2O2 were tested in pH = 4.5 and 7.4 buffer solutions, respectively (Fig. 2 k). It was found that LCDs-OA had almost no CAT-like activity under acidic conditions (pH = 4.5). The CAT-like activity of LCDs and LCDs-OA was investigated by monitoring dissolved oxygen concentrations over a wide pH range (pH = 4–10) (Fig. 2 l). LCDs and LCDs-OA dissolved oxygen concentrations were found to be nearly unchanged in the acidic range (pH = 4–6). Dissolved oxygen concentrations were consistently greater in the presence of LCDs-OA than in the presence of LCDs. LCDs-OA showed better CAT-like activity only at alkaline pH (pH = 7–10). This further opens up the possibility of subsequent adaptive therapies.

By comparing the XPS and FT-IR spectra of LCDs and LCDs-OA, it was observed that LCDs-OA exhibits higher levels of CC, CO, and O-CO, but lower levels of C-O. These compositional differences suggest a potential impact on the enzyme-like activity of LCDs-OA. Subsequently, LCDs-OA underwent treatment using NaBH4, NaOH, and 1,3-propanesultone (PS), resulting in LCDs-OA-NaBH4, LCDs-OA-NaOH, and LCDs-OA-PS, respectively. [36]The enzyme-mimicking properties (POD-like and CAT-like) of the treated materials were investigated and compared, as depicted in Fig. 3
a-d. Concurrently, the variations in functional groups due to different treatments were explored using FT-IR (Fig. 3 e), XPS high-resolution C 1 s spectra (Fig. 3 f-h) and 1H NMR spectra ( Fig. S9 ).The utilization of NaBH4 led to a reduction in carbonyl groups on the LCDs-OA nanozyme surface, accompanied by a significant increase in hydroxyl groups, indicative of carbonyl group reduction to hydroxyl groups (Fig. 3 f). Notably, the POD-like activity of LCDs-OA-NaBH4 saw a substantial decrease (76.2 %), while CAT-like activity experienced a milder reduction (30.5 %). This implies a significant inhibitory effect of hydroxyl groups on POD-like properties but a less pronounced impact on CAT-like properties. Moreover, carbonyl groups were identified as a crucial factor for LCDs-OA POD-like activity rather than CAT-like activity. Furthermore, NaOH treatment resulted in a decrease in carbonyl and carboxyl groups on the LCDs-OA nanozyme surface, coupled with an increase in CC content (Fig. 3 g). The POD-like and CAT-like activities of LCDs-OA-NaOH exhibited significant decreases (68.4 % and 48.6 %, respectively). It is proposed that CC enhances electron transfer efficiency, thereby improving activity, further supporting the inhibitory effect of hydroxyl groups on POD-like activity. Carboxyl groups were found to have a minimal effect on POD-like performance but played a crucial role in influencing CAT-like performance. Finally, the use of PS led to a decrease in hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the LCDs-OA nanozyme surface, accompanied by an increase in CC content (Fig. 3 h). The POD-like and CAT-like activities of LCDs-OA-PS decreased by 13.2 % and 67.9 %, respectively. A comparison of functional group changes suggests that the carboxyl group serves as the secondary non-major active site for POD-like properties and the primary active site for CAT-like properties.

Theoretical calculations were performed using the Vienna ab Initio Simulation Package (VASP) to determine the mechanism and energy of •OH and O2 formation from H2O2 at C, CO and COOH sites at different pH values, confirming the catalytic mechanism and consolidating the experimental results. As shown in Fig. 4
a-c, the transformation of H2O2 to •OH at different active sites under acidic conditions (pH = 4.5) undergoes the process: * + H2O2 → *H2O2 → 2*OH → •OH + *OH. The rate-determining step in this is the transformation of 2*OH to •OH + *OH. The energy barriers of C, CO and COOH are 1.19, 0.28 and 0.44 eV respectively indicating that the dissociation to •OH is easier on CO. During the reaction under acidic conditions, H2O2 is unstable and easily converted to 2*OH at both CO and COOH sites. This also proves the experimental result that CO is the primary active site and COOH is the secondary active site. Since the redox potential of H2O2/O2 decreases significantly with increasing pH, it tends to decompose H2O2 into O2. [61,62] Under alkaline conditions (pH = 7.4) the conversion of H2O2 to O2 at different active sites undergoes the process of * + H2O2 → *H2O2 → *H + *OOH→2*H + *O2. (Fig. 4 d-f) Where the stability of *OOH is an important factor in determining the thermodynamic stability of the whole reaction and the rate-determining step of the process is also in the transformation of *OOH to *O2. The energy barriers for this process on C, CO, and COOH are 2.24, 1.28, and 1.08 eV, respectively, indicating that *OOH decomposes more readily to 2*H and *O2 on COOH. Under alkaline conditions, there was no major difference in the energy barriers, so it was concluded that CO might be the secondary active site, which is consistent with the experimental results.

Experimental and theoretical calculations reveal the different effects of functional groups on POD-like and CAT-like activities. The Π-conjugated electron system in the CC structure enhances the enzyme-like activity by accelerating the rate of electron transfer. The electrons are transferred from the top of the valence band of graphene to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of H2O2.[63,64] It was observed that the hydroxyl group inhibits the peroxidase-like activity, while the carbonyl group becomes the major active site and the carboxyl group plays a lesser role which is in accordance with previous reports. [37,63] On the contrary, under alkaline conditions the redox potential of H2O2/O2 is lower (0.695 V) and the formation of anions from COOH accelerates electron transfer, making H2O2 more easily oxidized to O2.[65,66] Thus the carboxyl group is preferentially the primary active site while the carbonyl group plays a secondary role.

To explore the viability of utilizing organic acid treatment for enhancing lignin's POD-like and CAT-like properties, employed tartaric and citric acids in the treatment process, resulting in lignin carbon dots named LCDs-TA and LCDs-CA. As illustrated in Fig. S10-
12, both LCDs-TA and LCDs-CA exhibited improved properties compared to LCDs. However, a notable distinction persisted when compared to LCDs-OA, hypothesized to stem from the simplistic structure of oxalic acid and its higher carboxyl group content.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and nanozyme properties evaluation of LCDs-OA@CaO2
ce:section.ce:para:
CaO2 undergoes conversion to H2O2 under acidic conditions and releases oxygen under neutral and alkaline conditions. Firstly, we used the H2O2 fluorescent probe to detect the H2O2 produced by CaO2 at different pH. The relative fluorescence intensity of the emission at 514 nm proved that more H2O2 could be produced under acidic conditions, as shown in Fig. S13 . The production of O2 by CaO2 at different pH was examined using the dissolved oxygen meter. It was found that more O2 was produced under neutral/basic condition, as indicated by the oxygen concentration test. This demonstrated the feasibility of CaO2 for the smart H2O2/O2 self-sufficiency. To achieve self-supply of H2O2 and O2 during the treatment process, CaO2 was combined with LCDs-OA, and a series of experiments were conducted. The presence of CaO2 in the composite, LCDs-OA@CaO2, was confirmed through XRD analysis, where characteristic peaks of both CaO2 and LCDs-OA were observed ( Fig. S14
a). Further detailed analysis via FT-IR tests ( Fig. S14
b) revealed an additional characteristic peak of CaO2 (874 cm−1) in LCDs-OA@CaO2 after the complexation with LCDs-OA. Notably, the wave number of LCDs-OA remained largely unchanged after the composite formation, indicating minimal structural impact on LCDs-OA. These results affirm the successful preparation of LCDs-OA@CaO2.

The POD-like activity of the LCDs-OA@CaO2 nanozymes was evaluated using TMB as a chromogenic substrate ( Fig. S15
a–d). Remarkably, the LCDs-OA@CaO2 system demonstrated the ability to oxidize TMB under acidic conditions (pH = 4.5) even in the absence of added H2O2. This system exhibited comparable performance to the simultaneous presence of LCDs-OA and H2O2. Analyzing the dissolved O2 content in the system confirmed the LCDs-OA@CaO2 system's effective catalysis of H2O2 decomposition to produce O2 under neutral conditions (pH = 7.4). Notably, CaO2 further contributed to O2 production through its decomposition ( Fig. S15
e-g). The different enzymatic properties of the LCDs-OA@CaO2 system were assessed over a wide pH range (pH = 4–10) ( Fig. S15
h). Consistently, a similar pattern was observed as with LCDs-OA, where acidic conditions (pH = 4–6) exhibited POD-like properties, and alkaline conditions (pH = 7.4–10) displayed CAT-like characteristics. This suggests that the LCDs-OA@CaO2 system does not affect the intrinsic performance of LCDs-OA and is expected to be a platform for ROS-balanced therapy with H2O2 and O2 self-supply. Finally, stability tests were carried out to test the enzyme-like activity at days 0, 10, 20, 30, 60, 120, and 180 after the synthesis of the materials, and it was found that the materials still retained 81.3 % and 80.2 % of the POD and CAT-like activity at day 180 ( Fig. S16 ).

The antibacterial efficacy of LCDs-OA against DR bacteria in the presence of H2O2 was investigated, (multidrug-resistant Escherichia coli (MDR E. coli) and multidrug-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MDR S. aureus) were used as DR bacterial models). [67,68] Bacterial cells underwent treatment with LCDs-OA under varied conditions (Fig. 5
a) and concentrations (0, 20, 30, 40, and 50 μg·mL−1) (Fig. 5 b) for 4 h. Fig. 5 a-d illustrate that the antimicrobial effect was apparent only with both LCDs-OA and H2O2, showcasing a gradual reduction in bacterial (MDR E. coli and MDR S. aureus) colonies with increasing LCDs-OA concentration. At 50 μg·mL−1 LCDs-OA, MDR E. coli and MDR S. aureus colonies on LB agar plates vanished entirely. UV–vis spectroscopy assessed bacterial survival in LB liquid medium under various conditions (Fig. 5 e) and concentrations (Fig. 5 f) of LCDs-OA. Notably, when LCDs-OA concentration reached 40 μg·mL−1 and coexisted with H2O2, MDR E. coli and MDR S. aureus survival rates were merely 2.6 ± 0.1 % and 1.3 ± 0.2 %, respectively. Bacterial survival variation over time, measured by Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy ( Fig. S17 ), indicated LCDs-OA's effectiveness in inhibiting bacteria in the presence of H2O2, achieving significant inhibition within 30 min (survival rate < 70 %). These findings affirm that LCDs-OA, in the presence of H2O2, combats DR bacteria under acidic conditions.

The microstructure and morphology of cells were further examined using TEM before and after LCDs-OA treatment. In Fig. 5 g, untreated MDR E. coli displayed a typical rod-shaped morphology with clear boundaries and intact cell membranes. Post-LCDs-OA treatment, MDR E. coli cells were severely fragmented, and bacterial contents were released. Similar observations were made for MDR S. aureus. Untreated MDR S. aureus maintained intact spherical morphology with smooth cell membranes, while LCDs-OA-treated MDR S. aureus exhibited folds and damage. TEM results aligned with antibacterial experiment outcomes, consistent with previous evidence that LCDs-OA activates H2O2 to generate sufficient •OH, inducing oxidative stress and disrupting bacterial cell membranes for potent antibacterial effects.

The antibacterial properties of the LCDs-OA@CaO2 system were investigated at different concentrations (0, 50, 75, and 100 μg·mL−1) without the addition of H2O2 ( Fig. S18
a&b). Bacterial survival rates were also studied using the UV–vis spectroscopy method ( Fig. S18
c). At a concentration of LCDs-OA@CaO2 reached 75 μg·mL−1, the survival rates of MDR E. coli and MDR S. aureus were only 2.1 ± 0.2 % and 1.8 ± 0.4 %, respectively. The material concentration increase was attributed to the relative change in LCDs-OA concentration in the system, demonstrating the feasibility of the LCDs-OA@CaO2 system against drug-resistant bacteria under acidic conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and ROS scavenging ability in vitro
ce:section.ce:para:
NCM-460 cells served as a model to assess the cytotoxicity of LCDs-OA and LCDs-OA@CaO2 after a 24-hour incubation. The 3-[4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5 diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay results indicated that LCDs-OA exhibited no cytotoxicity at concentrations below 100 μg·mL−1, with slight cytotoxicity observed at concentrations exceeding 200 μg·mL−1 after 24 h of incubation (Fig. 6
a). LCDs-OA@CaO2 demonstrated no cytotoxicity at concentrations below 200 μg·mL−1 and exhibited slight cytotoxicity at concentrations exceeding 300 μg·mL−1 after 24 h of incubation (Fig. 6 b). Therefore, concentrations below 200 μg·mL−1 of LCDs-OA@CaO2 were chosen for subsequent studies. To evaluate the protective capacity against cellular oxidative damage, MTT and live/dead cell staining assays were employed. Results in Fig. 6 c&d showed a dose-dependent increase in cell viability after pretreatment with LCDs-OA and LCDs-OA@CaO2. In 25 μg·mL−1 LCDs-OA, approximately 43.8 % of cells were rescued from H2O2-induced death. Similarly, at 50 μg·mL−1 of LCDs-OA@CaO2, about 43.8 % of cells were rescued from H2O2-induced death. The increase in concentration was associated with the relative decrease in LCDs-OA amount in the system, a trend corroborated by live/dead cell staining (Fig. 6 e).

Intracellular ROS levels in H2O2-treated NCM-460 cells were further explored using the 2′,7′-Dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) method. As depicted in Fig. 7
a, the intracellular ROS level (green fluorescent signal) significantly increased after 1 mM H2O2 treatment. Pre-treatment with LCDs-OA@CaO2 dose-dependently reduced ROS levels, as validated by quantitative analysis through flow cytometry (Fig. 7 b&c). At a concentration of 50 μg·mL−1, LCDs-OA@CaO2 reversed relative ROS levels by 56.1 %, indicating its efficient ROS scavenging activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo antimicrobial and wound healing activity
ce:section.ce:para:
The developed LCDs-OA@CaO2 was subjected to in vivo experiments to validate its therapeutic efficacy for wound healing of drug-resistant bacteria. MDR E. coli and MDR S. aureus infected skin wound models were established in BALB/c female mice. As shown in Fig. 8
a, after the bacterial infection lasted for 24 h, the mice were randomly divided into three groups, and the traumatized areas were treated and counted on days 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10: (Ⅰ) control (PBS buffer), (Ⅱ) CaO2, and (Ⅲ) LCDs-OA@CaO2, respectively. Significant bacterial deposition and development of suppuration were seen throughout the process in both the control and CaO2 groups, illustrating the dilemma of relying solely on the immune system to clear bacteria. In addition, a longer period of time to clear the bacteria would hinder the upcoming wound healing. Notably, LCDs-OA@CaO2 treated wounds healed completely after 10 days, while wounds in the other groups, including the CaO2 group, did not heal. This is due to the fact that LCDs-OA@CaO2 adapted to the wound environment produces ROS to sterilize the bacteria in the early stage of infection and O2 to promote DR infected wound healing in the later stage. More specifically, as shown in Fig. 8 b, there was no significant difference in the body weight of mice in each group. However, wound healing and size reduction were observed after 8 days of LCDs-OA@CaO2 (91.8 %) combination treatment compared to CaO2 and control (70.3 % and 67.2 %, respectively) (Fig. 8 c). The results showed that LCDs-OA@CaO2 treated MDR E. coli and MDR S. aureus bacterial infected wounds healed best.

On Day 10, we collected skin samples from the vicinity of the infected wound and performed a pathological evaluation of each batch of samples to monitor specific microscopic changes in the wound during healing. By observing the images of hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining and the results of Masson staining (Fig. 8 d), it was clearly observed that the epidermis layer of the damaged area was regenerated in the LCDs-OA@CaO2 treatment group, while the dermis layer was repaired by abundant connective tissue. In contrast, the PBS and CaO2 groups still had significant skin surface damage with disorganized collagen grid structure and inflammatory cells everywhere. The development of collagen fibers in the wound was monitored by Masson staining, and a large number of fibroblasts were observed. There were fewer collagen fibers in the PBS and CaO2 groups and more collagen fibers in the LCDs-OA@CaO2 treated group. LCDs-OA@CaO2 showed different enzyme activities in response to the changes in the wound microenvironment and achieved rapid sterilization of the wound tissue after DR bacterial infection and removal of excess ROS from the wound, and alleviated the hypoxia at the wound site.

As can be seen in Fig. 8 e, H&E staining did not reveal any obvious organ damage, abnormality, or inflammation compared to the PBS groups, indicating that CaO2 and LCDs-OA@CaO2 had no obvious in vivo biotoxicity during therapy. In summary, LCDs-OA@CaO2 can be used as a biocompatible ROS-balanced nanomaterial for wound therapy of DR bacterial infections.

In summary, we constructed a series of LCDs by a simple and effective strategy using lignin as a raw material, among which LCDs-OA possessed excellent POD-like and CAT-like activities. The excellent dual enzyme-like activity opens up the possibility of applying LCDs-OA to ROS balance in DR bacterial wound therapy. The switching of CO and COOH active sites of LCDs-OA under acidic and alkaline conditions was demonstrated by enzyme-like activity studies and theoretical calculations. In in vitro experiments, it was observed that the POD-like activity-mediated ROS treatment of LCDs-OA was effective in killing bacteria, and the CAT-like activity-mediated ROS scavenging of LCDs-OA was effective in protecting cells, thus realizing the self-adaptive balance of ROS in the different phases of wound healing, and that LCDs-OA had good biocompatibility. Notably, an adaptive ROS balance therapeutic platform (LCDs-OA@CaO2) was constructed by combining CaO2 and LCDs-OA. The possibility and biocompatible of LCDs-OA@CaO2 applied to the therapy of DR bacterial infected wounds were further demonstrated by in vivo DR bacterial infected wound therapy experiments. This study provides a convenient and feasible strategy for the comprehensive therapy of drug-resistant bacterial infected wounds.
